notes: Dominant model for new installations; tabular row/column structure; SQL query language; flexible distributed architecture; decade of dominance

Also called Universal Servers; adds object/complex data type support to relational engine; Oracle8 launch 1997 drove ORDBMS to >50% of new DBMS revenue; 1998 is first year ORDBMS revenues outpace pure RDBMS
<$200M 1997 revenues; stores objects not just rows/columns; strong in telecom and finance verticals; assimilated into mainstream primarily through ORDBMSs
De facto standard on Unix servers; projected 1998 license revenues ~$2B; object-relational; Object-Relational DBMS flagship; Network Computing Architecture integration
Core Informix DBMS post-1997 relaunch; parallel architecture; optional DataBlade packages for Internet, OLAP, OLTP, user-defined types; excellent OLTP and DSS performance
RDBMS; best vertical scalability among major vendors; write-once-run-anywhere-across-tiers; combined with Powersoft tools, Jaguar CTS, PowerDynamo; no object/UDT support
Data warehousing; combined with Adaptive Server Enterprise provides excellent data warehousing foundation
Mobile and occasionally-connected deployments; also Adaptive Server for Windows CE and Ultralight
Growing >50% annually; majority share of Windows NT DBMS market; bundled with Microsoft Back Office; lacks row-level locking and object support in 6.5
Due H2 1998; Aberdeen calls it 'biggest DBMS story of 1998'; adds scalability, replication, data warehousing support, row-level locking, simplifies administration
Combines parallel-scalable and replication products with core ORDBMS; Extender technology for complex/UDTs; Data Joiner and Data Propagator; Java/Internet support
Relational half of CA DBMS strategy; mid-range Unix; excellent replication and distributed administration tools; part of Ingres II family
First enterprise ODBMS from major database vendor; pure object DBMS; Jasmine Studio visual development; appeals to enterprises not committed to ORDBMSs
Integrates Btrieve engine and Scalable.SQL; hybrid transactional/query for SMB embedded market
Oracle Lite 3.0 and Oracle Lite for Windows CE; small-footprint mobile DBMS
Aberdeen: explosive NT DBMS market growth to continue; NT clustering expected to fundamentally alter enterprise production market within 2-3 years; driving commoditization
Java support becoming required DBMS capability; JDBC/proprietary optimized drivers; ability to store Java objects and stored procedures; driving new mid-tier Web database segment
Core DBMS workload; Unix enterprise production segment slowing; mission-critical applications; high-performance requirement
One of 'hot' DBMS sub-segments; high-growth and high-profit in 1997; requires powerful query optimizers and parallel-scalable capabilities; Aberdeen bright spot
Second most heavily-used mainframe data management software; OLTP for large-scale datacenters; being gradually replaced by DB2
Large portion of corporate data worldwide stored here; fast for small-dataset OLTP; hard to administer; newer mainframes moving to DB2 ORDBMS
Cited as driver of mobile/occasionally-connected DBMS market; Aberdeen believes significant new niche market
